title: Eventual safe nodes
description: Find safe nodes using reverse graph

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Initialize state array of size 7 with all 0 (unvisited).
Step 2: Start DFS from node 0: mark 0 as visiting (1). Explore neighbor 1 (state 0) → DFS from 1: mark 1 as visiting. Explore neighbor 2 (state 0) → DFS from 2: mark 2 as visiting. Explore neighbor 5 (state 0) → DFS from 5: mark 5 as visiting. Node 5 has no neighbors, mark as visited (2). Back to node 2: all neighbors processed, mark 2 as visited. Back to node 1: explore neighbor 3 (state 0) → DFS from 3: mark 3 as visiting. Explore neighbor 0 (state 1, visiting) → cycle detected! Mark nodes 0,1,3 as unsafe. Backtrack: node 3 unsafe, node 1 unsafe, node 0 unsafe.
Step 3: DFS from node 4 (state 0): mark visiting, explore neighbor 5 (state 2, visited) → no cycle, mark 4 as visited (safe).
Step 4: Node 6 (state 0): no neighbors, mark visited (safe).
Step 5: Collect safe nodes: 2 (visited), 4 (visited), 5 (visited), 6 (visited).

Step 1: Initialize state array of size 5 with all 0.
Step 2: DFS from node 0: mark visiting. Explore neighbor 1 (state 0) → DFS from 1: mark visiting. Explore neighbor 1 (self-loop, state 1) → cycle detected! Mark 0 and 1 unsafe.
Step 3: DFS from node 2 (state 0): mark visiting. Explore neighbor 3 (state 0) → DFS from 3: mark visiting. Explore neighbor 0 (state unsafe, treated as visited), neighbor 4 (state 0) → DFS from 4: no neighbors, mark visited. Back to node 3: all neighbors processed, but node 0 is unsafe, so node 3 is unsafe. Back to node 2: neighbor 4 is safe, but neighbor 3 unsafe makes node 2 unsafe.
Step 4: Node 4 already visited (safe).
Step 5: Only node 4 is safe.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS
approaches.summary: Perform DFS from each node to detect cycles; a node is safe if no cycle is reachable from it. This is straightforward but inefficient due to repeated traversals.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DFS from each node to detect cycles and identify safe nodes.
ladders.explanation: The brute force DFS approach solves the eventual safe nodes problem by checking each node individually for cycle reachability. A node is considered 'safe' if no cycle is reachable from it through any path in the directed graph. The intuition is straightforward: perform a depth-first search from each node, marking visited nodes to detect cycles. If a cycle is detected during DFS from a starting node, that node and all nodes in the cycle are unsafe. Otherwise, the node is safe. The algorithm works by iterating through all nodes (0 to n-1), running DFS from each unclassified node. During DFS, we maintain three states: unvisited (0), visiting (1, currently in recursion stack), and visited (2, fully processed). If we encounter a node marked as 'visiting' during DFS, we've found a cycle. The time complexity is O(n²) in worst case because we might traverse the entire graph from each node (though visited nodes are skipped in subsequent traversals). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and state array. This approach is inefficient but conceptually clear, making it a good starting point before optimizing with memoization or reverse graph approaches. Common mistakes include not properly resetting state between DFS calls, missing cycle detection logic, or incorrect handling of visited nodes. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the DFS cycle detection subroutine.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize state array of size 7 with all 0 (unvisited).
Step 2: Start DFS from node 0: mark 0 as visiting (1). Explore neighbor 1 (state 0) → DFS from 1: mark 1 as visiting. Explore neighbor 2 (state 0) → DFS from 2: mark 2 as visiting. Explore neighbor 5 (state 0) → DFS from 5: mark 5 as visiting. Node 5 has no neighbors, mark as visited (2). Back to node 2: all neighbors processed, mark 2 as visited. Back to node 1: explore neighbor 3 (state 0) → DFS from 3: mark 3 as visiting. Explore neighbor 0 (state 1, visiting) → cycle detected!
... (simplified)

Step 1: Initialize state array of size 5 with all 0.
Step 2: DFS from node 0: mark visiting. Explore neighbor 1 (state 0) → DFS from 1: mark visiting. Explore neighbor 1 (self-loop, state 1) → cycle detected! Mark 0 and 1 unsafe.
Step 3: DFS from node 2 (state 0): mark visiting. Explore neighbor 3 (state 0) → DFS from 3: mark visiting. Explore neighbor 0 (state unsafe, treated as visited), neighbor 4 (state 0) → DFS from 4: no neighbors, mark visited. Back to node 3: all neighbors processed, but node 0 is unsafe, so node 3 is unsafe. Back to no
... (simplified)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Helper / BFS Sort
ladders.desc: Depth-first search function that detects cycles starting from a given node.
ladders.explanation: The DFS cycle detection subroutine is the core of the brute force approach. Given a starting node in a directed graph, it performs DFS to determine if any cycle is reachable from that node. The intuition uses a state machine: each node is unvisited (0), visiting (1, in current recursion stack), or visited (2, fully processed). When we encounter a node marked 'visiting' during DFS, we've found a back edge indicating a cycle. The algorithm recursively explores all neighbors: if any neighbor returns false (has cycle), the current node is unsafe; if all neighbors return true, the node is safe. Time complexity for one DFS call is O(V+E) for the subgraph reachable from the start node. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion depth. This subroutine connects to L0 by being called for each node in the main loop. Common mistakes include not checking for 'visiting' state before 'visited', infinite recursion in cycles, or not propagating cycle information correctly. The pseudocode: function dfs(node): if state[node] == 1: return false (cycle); if state[node] == 2: return true (already known safe); state[node] = 1; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not dfs(neighbor): return false; state[node] = 2; return true.

examples.trace:
Step 1: dfs(0): state[0]=0 → mark visiting (1). Explore neighbor 1.
Step 2: dfs(1): state[1]=0 → mark visiting. Explore neighbor 2.
Step 3: dfs(2): state[2]=0 → mark visiting. Explore neighbor 0.
Step 4: dfs(0): state[0]=1 (visiting) → cycle detected! Return false.
Step 5: Propagate back: dfs(2) returns false, dfs(1) returns false, dfs(0) returns false.

Step 1: dfs(4): state[4]=0 → mark visiting. Explore neighbor 5.
Step 2: dfs(5): state[5]=0 → mark visiting. No neighbors → mark visited (2), return true.
Step 3: Back to dfs(4): all neighbors processed → mark visited (2), return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Propagation / Graph Reversal
ladders.desc: Using three states (unvisited, visiting, visited) to detect cycles during DFS.
ladders.explanation: The three-state system is the central insight for efficient cycle detection in DFS. Instead of just visited/unvisited, we add a 'visiting' state to represent nodes currently in the recursion stack. This allows us to distinguish between a node that was fully processed earlier (visited) versus one that's part of the current path (visiting). When we encounter a node marked 'visiting', it means we've returned to a node in the current DFS path, indicating a cycle. The intuition: during DFS, we push nodes onto the recursion stack as we traverse; if we try to push a node already in the stack, we have a cycle. The 'visiting' state tracks this stack membership. Algorithm: initialize all nodes as unvisited (0). When entering a node in DFS, mark as visiting (1). After processing all neighbors, mark as visited (2). If we encounter a visiting node, return cycle detected. Time complexity for state management is O(1) per node visit. Space is O(n) for the state array. This connects to L1 as the mechanism inside DFS. Common mistakes: using only two states (missing cycles), not resetting states between independent DFS calls, or incorrect state transitions. Pseudocode: state = [0]*n; def dfs(u): if state[u]==1: return False; if state[u]==2: return True; state[u]=1; for v in graph[u]: if not dfs(v): return False; state[u]=2; return True.

examples.trace:
Step 1: state=[0,0,0]. dfs(0): state[0]=0 → set to 1 (visiting).
Step 2: Explore neighbor 1: dfs(1): state[1]=0 → set to 1.
Step 3: Explore neighbor 2: dfs(2): state[2]=0 → set to 1.
Step 4: Explore neighbor 0: dfs(0): state[0]=1 (visiting) → cycle detected, return False.

examples.output: No cycle, all nodes marked visited
examples.trace:
Step 1: state=[0,0,0,0]. dfs(0): set to 1.
Step 2: dfs(1): set to 1.
Step 3: dfs(2): set to 1.
Step 4: dfs(3): set to 1, no neighbors → set to 2, return True.
Step 5: Back to dfs(2): all neighbors processed → set to 2, return True.
Step 6: Similarly for dfs(1) and dfs(0): set to 2, return True.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Implementing depth-first search to traverse all reachable nodes from a starting point.
ladders.explanation: Recursive DFS traversal is the intermediate concept needed before adding cycle detection. Given a directed graph and a start node, DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. The intuition is to mimic walking through a maze: go deep first, then retreat when dead ends are reached. Algorithm: mark current node as visited, then recursively visit all unvisited neighbors. This ensures we traverse the entire connected component. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the reachable subgraph. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack. This connects to L2 by being the traversal framework where we add state management. Common mistakes: not handling disconnected graphs, infinite recursion in cyclic graphs without visited checks, or stack overflow for deep graphs. Pseudocode: visited = set(); def dfs(node): if node in visited: return; visited.add(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: dfs(neighbor). This building block is essential because cycle detection modifies this basic DFS with state tracking.

examples.trace:
Step 1: dfs(0): mark visited, explore neighbors 1 and 2.
Step 2: dfs(1): mark visited, explore neighbor 3.
Step 3: dfs(3): mark visited, no neighbors, return.
Step 4: Back to dfs(1): done, return.
Step 5: Back to dfs(0): explore neighbor 2.
Step 6: dfs(2): mark visited, explore neighbor 3 (already visited), return.
Step 7: Traversal order: 0→1→3→2.

examples.output: Visited nodes: 0,1,2 (infinite loop without cycle detection)
examples.trace:
Step 1: dfs(0): mark visited, explore neighbor 1.
Step 2: dfs(1): mark visited, explore neighbor 2.
Step 3: dfs(2): mark visited, explore neighbor 0 (already visited, so return).
Step 4: Without cycle detection, this completes, but graph has a cycle.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration / Outdegree
ladders.desc: Representing a directed graph using adjacency list and iterating through neighbors.
ladders.explanation: The adjacency list representation is fundamental for graph algorithms. A directed graph is represented as a list of lists, where graph[i] contains all nodes reachable from node i. This allows efficient neighbor iteration, crucial for DFS. Intuition: each node has a 'bucket' of its outgoing edges. For example, graph = [[1,2],[2],[0]] means node 0 points to 1 and 2, node 1 points to 2, node 2 points to 0. Operations: accessing graph[node] gives neighbors in O(1), iterating through them takes O(degree(node)). Space complexity is O(V+E). This connects to L3 as the data structure DFS operates on. Common mistakes: incorrect indexing (0-based vs 1-based), misunderstanding directed vs undirected, or not handling empty lists for nodes with no neighbors. Pseudocode: graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]; to add edge u→v: graph[u].append(v). In DFS, we use for neighbor in graph[node]: ... to explore.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize graph for 3 nodes: [[],[],[]].
Step 2: Add edge 0→1: graph[0].append(1) → [[1],[],[]].
Step 3: Add edge 0→2: graph[0].append(2) → [[1,2],[],[]].
Step 4: Add edge 1→2: graph[1].append(2) → [[1,2],[2],[]].
Step 5: Add edge 2→0: graph[2].append(0) → [[1,2],[2],[0]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of directed graphs, nodes, edges, and safe nodes.
ladders.explanation: A directed graph has nodes and directed edges. A node is eventual safe if every path from it leads to a terminal node (no outgoing edges) without cycles. Safe nodes are those from which no cycle is reachable.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Node 0 has outgoing edge to node 1.
Step 2: Node 1 has outgoing edge to node 2.
Step 3: Node 2 has no outgoing edges.
Step 4: Paths: 0→1, 1→2, 0→1→2.
Step 5: No cycles because no edge returns to a previous node.

Step 1: Node 0 points to node 1.
Step 2: Node 1 points back to node 0.
Step 3: This creates a cycle: 0→1→0.
Step 4: You can traverse indefinitely: 0→1→0→1...

approaches.name: DFS with Memoization
approaches.summary: Use DFS with states (unvisited, visiting, safe, unsafe) to mark nodes as safe or unsafe based on cycle detection. This avoids redundant work by caching results.

ladders.title: Full Problem — DFS with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all safe nodes in a directed graph using DFS with state tracking and memoization.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires identifying nodes that are 'safe'—nodes from which all possible paths eventually terminate at a node with no outgoing edges (terminal nodes). The key insight is that nodes are unsafe if they're part of a cycle or reach a cycle. Using DFS with memoization avoids redundant work by tracking node states: UNVISITED (0), VISITING (1), SAFE (2), UNSAFE (3). Starting DFS from each node, we mark nodes as VISITING during traversal. If we encounter a VISITING node, we've found a cycle, marking all nodes in that path as UNSAFE. If all neighbors are SAFE, the node is SAFE. Memoization ensures each node is processed once, achieving O(n) time. The intuition: safety propagates backward from terminal nodes—nodes with no outgoing edges are inherently safe, and nodes whose all neighbors are safe become safe. This approach efficiently handles cycles by marking entire cycles as unsafe. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes plus edges, as each node and edge is visited once. Space complexity is O(n) for the adjacency list, state array, and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This level integrates the DFS helper function to compute safety states for all nodes. Common mistakes include not handling cycles correctly (infinite recursion) or missing memoization, leading to O(n²) time. Real code pattern: Initialize state array, define DFS function that returns bool (safe/unsafe), iterate nodes, collect safe ones.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize state for 7 nodes: [0,0,0,0,0,0,0]. Step 2: DFS from node 0: state[0]=1, neighbors 1,2. DFS node 1: state[1]=1, neighbors 2,3. DFS node 2: state[2]=1, neighbors 5. DFS node 5: state[5]=1, neighbors [] (terminal), mark safe: state[5]=2, return True. Node 2: all neighbors safe, state[2]=2, return True. Node 1: neighbor 3: DFS node 3: state[3]=1, neighbors 0. Node 0 is VISITING (state=1), cycle detected, mark unsafe: state[3]=3, return False. Node 1: has unsafe neighbor, state[1]=3, return False. Node 0: has unsafe neighbor, sta
... (simplified)

Step 1: Initialize state for 5 nodes: [0,0,0,0,0]. Step 2: DFS from node 0: state[0]=1, neighbors 1,2,3,4. DFS node 1: state[1]=1, neighbors 1,2 (self-loop and cycle). DFS node 1 again (VISITING), cycle, state[1]=3, return False. Node 0: has unsafe neighbor, state[0]=3, return False. Step 3: DFS node 2: state[2]=1, neighbors 3,4. DFS node 3: state[3]=1, neighbors 0,4. Node 0 is UNSAFE (state=3), so node 3 unsafe: state[3]=3, return False. Node 2: has unsafe neighbor, state[2]=3, return False. Step 4: DFS node 4: state[4]=1, neighbors [] (termin
... (simplified)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Helper / BFS Sort
ladders.desc: Implement DFS function that determines if a node is safe using state memoization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core DFS function that recursively explores neighbors to determine node safety. It uses a state array: UNVISITED (0), VISITING (1), SAFE (2), UNSAFE (3). The function takes a node index, marks it as VISITING, then recursively processes each neighbor. If a neighbor is VISITING, a cycle is detected, and the current node is marked UNSAFE. If a neighbor is UNSAFE, the current node becomes UNSAFE. If all neighbors are SAFE, the node is marked SAFE. Memoization is achieved by returning the cached state (SAFE/UNSAFE) without re-computation. The intuition: DFS traverses paths to identify cycles or safe termination. Time complexity per call is O(k) where k is the number of neighbors, but with memoization, each node is processed once, so total O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for state storage and recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called for each node in the main solution to populate safety states. Common mistakes include not checking for VISITING state (leading to infinite recursion) or not caching results (causing exponential time). Real code pattern: Define dfs(node): if state[node] != UNVISITED: return state[node] == SAFE; state[node] = VISITING; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not dfs(neighbor): state[node] = UNSAFE; return False; state[node] = SAFE; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: state[0]=0 (UNVISITED), mark VISITING: state[0]=1. Step 2: neighbor 1: dfs(1). state[1]=0, mark VISITING: state[1]=1. Step 3: neighbor 2: dfs(2). state[2]=0, mark VISITING: state[2]=1. Step 4: neighbor 0: state[0]=1 (VISITING), cycle detected, mark UNSAFE: state[2]=3, return False. Step 5: Node 1: neighbor returned False, mark UNSAFE: state[1]=3, return False. Step 6: Node 0: neighbor returned False, mark UNSAFE: state[0]=3, return False.

Step 1: state[1]=0, mark VISITING: state[1]=1. Step 2: neighbor 0: dfs(0). state[0]=0, mark VISITING: state[0]=1. Step 3: neighbor [] (terminal), all neighbors safe, mark SAFE: state[0]=2, return True. Step 4: Node 1: all neighbors safe, mark SAFE: state[1]=2, return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Propagation / Graph Reversal
ladders.desc: Understand how safety states propagate and cycles are identified using DFS.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the logic of state transitions: a node is SAFE if all its neighbors are SAFE, and UNSAFE if any neighbor is UNSAFE or part of a cycle. The VISITING state is crucial for cycle detection: during DFS, if we encounter a node marked VISITING, it means we're in a recursive call stack that revisits the node, indicating a cycle. All nodes in that cycle are then marked UNSAFE. The propagation works backward: terminal nodes (no outgoing edges) are inherently SAFE, and safety flows to nodes whose all outgoing paths lead to SAFE nodes. The intuition is similar to topological sorting but for safety in directed graphs. Time and space analysis: O(n) for processing states, as each node's state is determined once. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the DFS helper function's condition checks. Common mistakes include incorrect state transitions (e.g., marking a node SAFE prematurely) or not propagating UNSAFE states through cycles. Real code pattern: Use if-else conditions to check neighbor states and update current node accordingly.

examples.input: States: node 0: VISITING, neighbors [1,2]; node 1: VISITING, neighbor [0]; node 2: UNVISITED
examples.output: Cycle detected at node 0-1, mark UNSAFE
examples.trace: Step 1: Node 0 is VISITING. Step 2: Process neighbor 1: node 1 is VISITING (encountered in same DFS path), cycle detected. Step 3: Mark node 0 and node 1 as UNSAFE. Step 4: Node 2 is not involved in cycle, processed separately.

examples.input: States: node 3: UNVISITED, neighbors [4]; node 4: SAFE, neighbors []
examples.trace: Step 1: Node 3 is UNVISITED, mark VISITING. Step 2: Check neighbor 4: state is SAFE. Step 3: All neighbors are SAFE, so mark node 3 as SAFE.

testCases.input: node state: UNVISITED, neighbor states: [SAFE, SAFE]
node state: UNVISITED, neighbor states: [SAFE, UNSAFE]
node state: VISITING, neighbor states: [UNVISITED, SAFE]
node state: VISITING, neighbor states: [VISITING, SAFE]
node state: UNVISITED, neighbor states: [UNVISITED, SAFE]
node state: UNVISITED, neighbor states: [SAFE, SAFE, SAFE]

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Learn to represent a directed graph and perform DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This level covers representing a graph as an adjacency list (list of lists) and implementing DFS traversal. An adjacency list is efficient for sparse graphs, storing for each node a list of its neighbors. DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, using recursion or a stack. The intuition: DFS mimics exploring paths in a maze, going as far as possible before trying alternatives. For this problem, DFS helps traverse outgoing edges to check for cycles or safe termination. Time complexity: O(V + E) for traversing all nodes and edges. Space complexity: O(V) for visited set and recursion stack. Connection to L2: DFS traversal is used to apply state propagation logic. Common mistakes include incorrect graph representation (e.g., using adjacency matrix for large graphs) or infinite recursion in cycles without cycle detection. Real code pattern: graph = adjacency list; visited = set; def dfs(node): if node in visited: return; visited.add(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: dfs(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited. Step 2: Neighbor 1: dfs(1), mark visited. Step 3: Neighbor 2: dfs(2), mark visited. Step 4: Neighbor 3: dfs(3), mark visited. Step 5: Backtrack: node 2 done, node 1 done, node 0 done.

examples.output: DFS order: 0,1,2 (cycle detected if no visited check)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited. Step 2: Neighbor 1: dfs(1), mark visited. Step 3: Neighbor 2: dfs(2), mark visited. Step 4: Neighbor 0: already visited, skip to avoid infinite loop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration / Outdegree
ladders.desc: Practice accessing and processing neighbors of a node in a graph.
ladders.explanation: This level involves the fundamental operation of iterating through the neighbors of a node in a graph represented as an adjacency list. Given a node index i, graph[i] returns a list of neighbor indices. Iteration is typically done with a for-loop. The intuition: to explore a graph, we need to examine all connections from a node. This operation is essential for DFS, BFS, and other graph algorithms. Time complexity: O(k) where k is the number of neighbors. Space complexity: O(1) for iteration. Connection to L3: This operation is used within DFS traversal to visit each neighbor. Common mistakes include index errors (e.g., accessing graph[-1]) or misunderstanding directed vs. undirected neighbors. Real code pattern: for neighbor in graph[node]: process(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: Access graph[0] = [1,2,3]. Step 2: Iterate: neighbor 1, then 2, then 3. Step 3: For each neighbor, print or process.
Step 1: Access graph[1] = [0,2]. Step 2: Iterate: neighbor 0, then 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of directed graphs, nodes, edges, and safe nodes.
ladders.explanation: A directed graph has nodes and directed edges. A node is eventual safe if every path from it leads to a terminal node (no outgoing edges) without cycles. Safe nodes are those from which no cycle is reachable.

examples.trace: Step 1: Node 0 has outgoing edge to node 1. Step 2: Node 1 has outgoing edge to node 2. Step 3: Node 2 has outgoing edge to node 0, forming a cycle.
Step 1: Node 0 points to 1 and 2. Step 2: Node 1 points to 3. Step 3: Node 2 points to 3. Step 4: Node 3 has no outgoing edges, so it's terminal.

approaches.name: Reverse Graph Topological Sort
approaches.summary: Reverse the graph and perform topological sort starting from terminal nodes (no outgoing edges). Nodes reachable from terminals in the reverse graph are safe.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reverse Graph Topological Sort
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all safe nodes in a directed graph by reversing edges and performing topological sort from terminal nodes.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires identifying all eventual safe nodes in a directed graph. A node is eventual safe if all possible paths starting from that node lead to a terminal node (node with no outgoing edges) or to another safe node. The key insight is that safety propagates backwards through the graph: if all nodes that can reach a node are safe, then that node is safe. By reversing the graph, we can start from terminal nodes (which are inherently safe) and work backwards using topological sort. In the reversed graph, edges point from nodes that were originally pointed to, toward nodes that originally pointed to them. This allows us to propagate safety from terminals backward through the original graph. The algorithm: 1) Build adjacency lists for both original and reversed graphs. 2) Calculate outdegree for each node in original graph to identify terminal nodes (outdegree=0). 3) Initialize a queue with all terminal nodes. 4) Perform BFS on reversed graph: for each node dequeued, mark as safe; for each neighbor in reversed graph (which are nodes that originally pointed to current node), decrement their outdegree (in original graph); if outdegree becomes 0, enqueue. 5) After BFS, collect all nodes marked safe. Time: O(V+E) for building graphs and BFS. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency lists and queue. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into complete solution. Common mistakes: Not handling cycles correctly (this approach naturally handles them by only queuing nodes when outdegree hits 0), confusing original vs reversed graph edges. Real pattern: BFS topological sort on reversed graph starting from terminals.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build original adjacency: 0->[1,2], 1->[2,3], 2->[5], 3->[0], 4->[5], 5->[], 6->[]. Reversed: 0->[3], 1->[0], 2->[0,1], 3->[1], 4->[], 5->[2,4], 6->[]. Step 2: Calculate outdegree: [2,2,1,1,1,0,0]. Step 3: Initialize queue with terminals (outdegree=0): [5,6]. Step 4: BFS on reversed: Dequeue 5, mark safe. Neighbors in reversed: 2,4. Decrement outdegree: 2:1->0 (enqueue 2), 4:1->0 (enqueue 4). Dequeue 6, mark safe. Neighbors: none. Dequeue 2, mark safe. Neighbors: 0,1. Decrement: 0:2->1, 1:2->1. Dequeue 4, mark safe. Neighbors: none. Queue empty. Step 5: Safe nodes: [2,4,5,6] sorted.

Step 1: Original: 0->[1,2,3,4], 1->[1,2], 2->[3,4], 3->[0,4], 4->[]. Reversed: 0->[3], 1->[0,1], 2->[0,1], 3->[0,2], 4->[0,2,3]. Step 2: Outdegree: [4,2,2,2,0]. Step 3: Queue: [4]. Step 4: BFS: Dequeue 4, mark safe. Neighbors: 0,2,3. Decrement: 0:4->3, 2:2->1, 3:2->1. Queue empty (no new outdegree=0). Step 5: Safe nodes: [4].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Helper / BFS Sort
ladders.desc: Perform BFS topological sort on reversed graph beginning with terminal nodes to propagate safety.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core algorithmic engine that identifies safe nodes. Given the reversed graph adjacency list and the outdegree array from the original graph, we perform BFS starting from all nodes with outdegree 0 (terminal nodes in original graph). The intuition: In the reversed graph, when we process a node, it means that node is safe (either terminal or all its outgoing edges in original graph lead to safe nodes). By traversing its neighbors in the reversed graph (which are nodes that originally pointed to it), we effectively say 'if you point to a safe node, and all your other outgoing edges also lead to safe nodes, then you become safe'. We track this by decrementing the outdegree of each neighbor (in original graph); when a node's outdegree hits 0, it means all nodes it points to are safe, so it becomes safe and gets enqueued. The algorithm: 1) Initialize queue with all nodes where outdegree[i]==0. 2) While queue not empty: dequeue node u, mark as safe. For each neighbor v in reversed_graph[u]: decrement outdegree[v]; if outdegree[v]==0, enqueue v. 3) Return all marked safe nodes. Time: O(V+E) for BFS traversal. Space: O(V) for queue and safe markers. Connection: This sub-routine is called within L0 after graph reversal. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark dequeued nodes as safe, not using outdegree array from original graph, incorrect BFS termination. Real pattern: Standard BFS with conditional enqueue based on outdegree.

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue init with outdegree=0: [5,6]. Safe set empty. Step 2: Dequeue 5, mark safe {5}. Neighbors in reversed[5]: [2,4]. Decrement outdegree: 2:1->0 (enqueue 2), 4:1->0 (enqueue 4). Step 3: Dequeue 6, mark safe {5,6}. Neighbors: []. Step 4: Dequeue 2, mark safe {2,5,6}. Neighbors: [0,1]. Decrement: 0:2->1, 1:2->1. Step 5: Dequeue 4, mark safe {2,4,5,6}. Neighbors: []. Queue empty. Return sorted safe list.
Step 1: Queue init: [4]. Safe set empty. Step 2: Dequeue 4, mark safe {4}. Neighbors: [0,2,3]. Decrement outdegree: 0:4->3, 2:2->1, 3:2->1. No new outdegree=0. Queue empty. Return [4].

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Propagation / Graph Reversal
ladders.desc: Reverse all edges in the graph to enable backward propagation of safety from terminal nodes.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that safety can be determined by working backwards from terminal nodes. In the original graph, if we want to know if node A is safe, we need to check all nodes reachable from A. But by reversing the graph, we transform the problem: instead of looking forward from A, we look backward from terminals. In the reversed graph, an edge from B to A means that in the original graph, A pointed to B. This reversal allows us to use topological sort starting from terminals: when we process a node in the reversed graph, we are essentially saying 'this node is safe', and we then notify all nodes that point to it in the original graph (its neighbors in reversed graph) that one of their outgoing edges leads to a safe node. The algorithm: Given original adjacency list, create new reversed adjacency list. For each node u, for each neighbor v in original[u], add u to reversed[v]. This takes O(V+E) time and O(V+E) space. Connection: This reversed graph is passed to L1's BFS routine. Common mistakes: Creating reversed graph incorrectly (missing nodes, wrong edge direction), not handling empty graphs. Real pattern: Nested loop over original adjacency to build reversed adjacency.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize reversed as list of 7 empty lists. Step 2: For node 0, neighbors [1,2]: add 0 to reversed[1] and reversed[2]. Step 3: Node 1, neighbors [2,3]: add 1 to reversed[2] and reversed[3]. Step 4: Node 2, neighbors [5]: add 2 to reversed[5]. Step 5: Node 3, neighbors [0]: add 3 to reversed[0]. Step 6: Node 4, neighbors [5]: add 4 to reversed[5]. Step 7: Nodes 5,6 have no neighbors. Result: reversed[0]=[3], [1]=[0], [2]=[0,1], [3]=[1], [4]=[], [5]=[2,4], [6]=[].

Step 1: Initialize 5 empty lists. Step 2: Node 0: add 0 to reversed[1], reversed[2], reversed[3], reversed[4]. Step 3: Node 1: add 1 to reversed[1], reversed[2]. Step 4: Node 2: add 2 to reversed[3], reversed[4]. Step 5: Node 3: add 3 to reversed[0], reversed[4]. Step 6: Node 4: no neighbors. Result: reversed[0]=[3], [1]=[0,1], [2]=[0,1], [3]=[0,2], [4]=[0,2,3].

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Calculate outdegree for each node to find terminal nodes (no outgoing edges).
ladders.explanation: Terminal nodes are nodes with no outgoing edges in the original graph. These are inherently safe because they have no paths leading to cycles. To find them, we compute the outdegree array: outdegree[i] = number of outgoing edges from node i. This is done by simply taking the length of each adjacency list in the original graph. The intuition: Outdegree is crucial because in the safety propagation algorithm, a node becomes safe only when its outdegree drops to 0 (meaning all nodes it points to are safe). Terminal nodes start with outdegree 0 and are the seeds for the BFS. Algorithm: Initialize outdegree array of size n with zeros. For each node i from 0 to n-1, set outdegree[i] = len(graph[i]). Time: O(V) to iterate through nodes. Space: O(V) for the array. Connection: This outdegree array is used in L1's BFS to initialize the queue and track when nodes become safe. Common mistakes: Confusing outdegree with indegree, not handling empty adjacency lists correctly. Real pattern: Simple array assignment based on adjacency list lengths.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize outdegree array size 7. Step 2: Node 0: len([1,2])=2 → outdegree[0]=2. Step 3: Node 1: len([2,3])=2 → outdegree[1]=2. Step 4: Node 2: len([5])=1 → outdegree[2]=1. Step 5: Node 3: len([0])=1 → outdegree[3]=1. Step 6: Node 4: len([5])=1 → outdegree[4]=1. Step 7: Node 5: len([])=0 → outdegree[5]=0. Step 8: Node 6: len([])=0 → outdegree[6]=0.

Step 1: Initialize size 5. Step 2: Node 0: len([1,2,3,4])=4 → outdegree[0]=4. Step 3: Node 1: len([1,2])=2 → outdegree[1]=2. Step 4: Node 2: len([3,4])=2 → outdegree[2]=2. Step 5: Node 3: len([0,4])=2 → outdegree[3]=2. Step 6: Node 4: len([])=0 → outdegree[4]=0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration / Outdegree
ladders.desc: Construct adjacency list representation of a directed graph given as a list of edges from each node.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list is a fundamental graph representation where each node has a list of its neighbors (nodes it points to). In this problem, the input is already in this form: graph[i] is a list of nodes that node i points to. However, we need to understand this structure to build the reversed graph. The operation involves creating a new list of lists for the reversed graph. For each node u, iterate through its neighbors v in the original graph, and add u to the list for v in the reversed graph. This requires careful indexing: ensure the reversed list has the same number of nodes as the original. The intuition: Adjacency lists allow efficient graph traversal (O(1) access to neighbors). Building the reversed graph is essentially transposing the adjacency matrix. Algorithm: 1) Initialize reversed_adj = [list() for _ in range(n)]. 2) For u in range(n): for v in graph[u]: reversed_adj[v].append(u). Time: O(V+E) to iterate through all edges. Space: O(V+E) for the new adjacency list. Connection: This operation is used in L2 to create the reversed graph. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds if graph size mismatches, not initializing empty lists properly. Real pattern: Nested loop with append to build reversed adjacency.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize reversed as 7 empty lists. Step 2: u=0, neighbors [1,2]: append 0 to reversed[1] and reversed[2]. Step 3: u=1, neighbors [2,3]: append 1 to reversed[2] and reversed[3]. Step 4: u=2, neighbors [5]: append 2 to reversed[5]. Step 5: u=3, neighbors [0]: append 3 to reversed[0]. Step 6: u=4, neighbors [5]: append 4 to reversed[5]. Step 7: u=5,6: no neighbors, no appends.

Step 1: Initialize 5 empty lists. Step 2: u=0, neighbors [1,2,3,4]: append 0 to reversed[1], reversed[2], reversed[3], reversed[4]. Step 3: u=1, neighbors [1,2]: append 1 to reversed[1] and reversed[2]. Step 4: u=2, neighbors [3,4]: append 2 to reversed[3] and reversed[4]. Step 5: u=3, neighbors [0,4]: append 3 to reversed[0] and reversed[4]. Step 6: u=4: no neighbors.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of directed graphs, nodes, edges, and safe nodes.
ladders.explanation: A directed graph has nodes and directed edges. A node is eventual safe if every path from it leads to a terminal node (no outgoing edges) without cycles. Safe nodes are those from which no cycle is reachable.

examples.output: Interpretation: 7 nodes. Node 0 -> 1,2; Node 1 -> 2,3; Node 2 -> 5; Node 3 -> 0; Node 4 -> 5; Node 5 -> none; Node 6 -> none.
examples.trace: Step 1: There are 7 lists, so 7 nodes indexed 0 to 6. Step 2: graph[0] = [1,2] means node 0 has edges to nodes 1 and 2. Step 3: graph[1] = [2,3] means node 1 points to 2 and 3. Step 4: graph[2] = [5] means node 2 points to 5. Step 5: graph[3] = [0] means node 3 points to 0. Step 6: graph[4] = [5] means node 4 points to 5. Step 7: graph[5] = [] means node 5 has no outgoing edges. Step 8: graph[6] = [] means node 6 has no outgoing edges.

examples.output: Interpretation: 5 nodes. Node 0 -> 1,2,3,4; Node 1 -> 1,2; Node 2 -> 3,4; Node 3 -> 0,4; Node 4 -> none.
examples.trace: Step 1: 5 nodes (0-4). Step 2: graph[0]=[1,2,3,4]: node 0 points to 1,2,3,4. Step 3: graph[1]=[1,2]: node 1 points to 1 (self-loop) and 2. Step 4: graph[2]=[3,4]: node 2 points to 3 and 4. Step 5: graph[3]=[0,4]: node 3 points to 0 and 4. Step 6: graph[4]=[]: node 4 has no outgoing edges.

testCases.expected: 4 nodes: all have no outgoing edges
